what_it_does: Registers a single MCP tool named dials that exposes and mutates adjustable character traits (dials) for an entity. It validates the op/dial/value arguments and delegates all state access to suite.dial_state and suite.set_dial.
observations: The file contains one public function register(mcp, suite) and a nested tool function dials(...).
The dials tool supports three string ops: list, describe, and set.
Setting a dial accepts a flat value and/or a list of condition-scoped overrides.
All state reads and writes are delegated to methods on the suite object.
Docstrings frame dials as reversible, declarative configuration rather than one-time decisions.
standouts: The overrides parameter is stored and validated now but intentionally not evaluated until a future rules-engine wiring exists.
The design explicitly names a human operator ('Tim') as the intended knob-turner.
The tool is self-describing: list and describe ops expose dial metadata and positions.
conventions: String-based op dispatch with explicit ValueError for missing or unknown cases.
Nested function definition for the tool handler.
Docstring-driven user-facing documentation embedded in code.
concerns: No local validation that value matches a declared dial position; suite is assumed to enforce it.
Overrides are accepted as raw dicts and stored without local schema validation.
The describe op returns whatever suite.dial_state(dial) produces; behavior depends on suite implementation.
notes: This is a thin, declarative MCP tool wrapper around a suite-level dial registry and persistence seam.
questions: What schema do overrides entries expect (e.g., required keys in each {when, value} dict)?
How does suite.dial_state distinguish between listing all dials and describing one?
Where are dial definitions (dials/<id>.py rows) discovered and what positions does each dial allow?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches actual behavior: it exposes adjustable character dials and delegates state to suite methods, with list/describe/set ops as advertised.
intention_signals: The file tries to make character traits feel like adjustable, reversible knobs that can be tuned by a human and later governed by rules-engine conditions, while keeping the tool surface minimal and self-documenting.
novelty: The explicit framing of character traits as dials with stored condition-scoped overrides that are validated but not yet evaluated, bridging immediate flat values and future rules-driven behavior.
contribution: A self-documenting MCP tool surface for listing, inspecting, and setting adjustable entity traits, plus a placeholder for condition-scoped overrides that a fused system can wire to a rules engine.
summary_for_embedding: mcp_face/tools/dials.py registers the dials MCP tool for listing, describing, and setting adjustable character traits, delegating state to suite.dial_state and suite.set_dial and accepting flat values plus condition-scoped overrides.
intention_for_embedding: Provide a reversible, human-tunable configuration surface for entity character traits with hooks for future rules-engine conditional overrides.

symbols.does: Wires the dials MCP tool into the server by decorating the nested dials function.
symbols.description: register(mcp, suite) is the module's public entry point. It applies the @mcp.tool() decorator to the nested dials function, binding the tool to the MCP server and passing the suite dependency into the closure so the tool can delegate state operations.

symbols.does: Handles list, describe, and set operations for adjustable character dials.
symbols.description: dials(op, dial, value, overrides) is the MCP tool implementation. It dispatches on op, validates required arguments, and forwards list/describe requests to suite.dial_state and set requests to suite.set_dial. It supports both a flat position value and a list of condition-scoped overrides that are stored now but evaluated later once rules-engine wiring exists.
